content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
April 11,
1995
James D. Petrikin
Councilmember
City of Fountain Valley
11160 Warner Avenue,
Suite 303
Fountain Valley,
CA
92708
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
1—95—104
Dear Mr. Petrikin:
You have requested advice regarding the conflict—of—interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”)
1 as they
pertain to your position as a councilmember in the City of
Fountain Valley.
Since you have not provided any facts regarding
a specific governmental decision,
we are rendering informal
assistance.
FACTS
Your wife is one of the owners of a company thatrepresents
long distance telephone carriers.
The company is presently
interested in seeking an agreement to provide long distance
service to the City of Fountain Valley.
The agreement with the
city would not require the approval of the city council because
the amount is less than that required for council participation.
Therefore, the staff has the authority to make the decision.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,
participating in,
or using his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he
has a financial interest.
An official has a financial interest in
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18954.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section
18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


File No.
1—95—104
Page
2
a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect
on the public generally,
on the official or a member of his
immediate family, or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth one
thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand
dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(C)
Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institution in
the regular course of business on terms available to the
public without regard to official status, aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to, received by or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
(e)
Any donor of,
or any intermediary or agent for
a donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating two hundred
fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by,
or promised to the public official within
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.
Section 87103.
Accordingly, you may not make, participate in making, or
attempt to use your official position to influence a governmental
decision if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally,
on any of your economic interests,
including the
company in which your wife has an ownership interest.
An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not
required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is
not reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.198.)
You stated that the long distance service agreement would not
require the approval of the city council and that you would not
participate in the decisionmaking process.
Please see Regulations
18700 and 18700.1, copies enclosed, for a broad definition of what
the decisionmaking process entails under the Act.
Regulation
18700 defines “making a governmental decision” and “participating
in the making of a governmental decision.”
Regulation 18700.1
defines “using official position to influence a governmental


File No.
1—95—104
Page
3
decision.”
You may not participate in any of the activities
enumerated in either of these regulations if there will be a
material financial effect on any of your economic interests.
Additionally, there may be other conflict-of-interest
provisions of law which may apply to your situation.
Please
consult the Attorney General’s Office regarding Government Code
Section 1090.
I trust this gives you some guidance
Sincerely,
SGC:JS: ak
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
.1,
Legal Division
Enclosures

sections.question: Your wife is one of the owners of a company that represents long distance telephone carriers. The company is presently interested in seeking an agreement to provide long distance service to the City of Fountain Valley. The agreement with the city would not require the approval of the city council because the amount is less than that required for council participation. Therefore, the staff has the authority to make the decision.
sections.conclusion: You may not make, participate in making, or attempt to use your official position to influence a governmental decision if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on any of your economic interests, including the company in which your wife has an ownership interest. You may not participate in any activities defined as making a governmental decision or participating in making a governmental decision if there will be a material financial effect on any of your economic interests.

the amount is less than that required for council participation.
Therefore, the staff has the authority to make the decision.

Please see Regulations
18700 and 18700.1, copies enclosed, for a broad definition of what
the decisionmaking process entails under the Act.
Regulation
18700 defines “making a governmental decision” and “participating
in the making of a governmental decision.”
Regulation 18700.1
defines “using official position to influence a governmental


File No.
1—95—104
decision.”
You may not participate in any of the activities
enumerated in either of these regulations if there will be a
material financial effect on any of your economic interests.

sections.question_synthetic: As a city councilmember, can the requestor participate in or influence a governmental decision regarding a long distance service agreement with the city when his wife owns a company interested in providing that service?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The requestor cannot make, participate in, or influence any governmental decision regarding the long distance service agreement because it would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his wife's company, which constitutes a financial interest under Section 87100. The requestor must avoid all activities defined as participating in the governmental decision-making process under Regulations 18700 and 18700.1.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: This is an informal advice letter (not providing immunity under Section 83114). The document includes clear FACTS and ANALYSIS sections. The question addresses a specific factual scenario regarding conflict of interest provisions. Minor formatting inconsistencies in original document do not affect extraction quality.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Your wife is one of the owners of a company that represents long distance telephone carriers. The company is presently interested in seeking an agreement to provide long distance service to the City of Fountain Valley. The agreement with the city would not require the approval of the city council because the amount is less than that required for council participation. Therefore, the staff has the authority to make the decision.

CONCLUSION: You may not make, participate in making, or attempt to use your official position to influence a governmental decision if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on any of your economic interests, including the company in which your wife has an ownership interest. You may not participate in any activities defined as making a governmental decision or participating in making a governmental decision if there will be a material financial effect on any of your economic interests.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises a city councilmember that he has a financial interest in his wife's long distance telephone carrier company and therefore cannot participate in or influence a governmental decision regarding a proposed service agreement between that company and the city. The advice notes that while the decision does not require city council approval, the requestor must still avoid all participation in the decision-making process under the Political Reform Act.
